title: Understand What You Built
tagline: Now that it works, learn why.

lessons.title: Decode the Secret Language Browsers Speak

accomplishments: Build a webpage that shows HTML vs CSS vs JavaScript
See exactly what each language does by turning them on and off
Create a visual demonstration you can show to anyone

do.title: Build a three-language demo page
steps: Create a new file called three-languages.html
Copy and paste the code below
do.code:
This heading is BIG and BLUE

This paragraph has a yellow background
do.result: You just saw HTML (structure), CSS (style), and JavaScript (behavior) working together!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the blue heading, yellow paragraph, AND the popup after clicking the button.

concepts.name: HTML (HyperText Markup Language)
concepts.definition:
This is the content and structure. The

,

, and
concepts.analogy: The frame and walls of a house. You need them first before you can paint or add electricity.

concepts.name: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)
concepts.definition: This is the visual design. The part between <style> tags made the heading blue and the paragraph yellow.
concepts.analogy: Paint, wallpaper, and interior design. CSS makes things look good.

concepts.name: JavaScript
concepts.definition: This is the behavior and interactivity. The onclick="alert('...')" part made the button DO something when clicked.
concepts.analogy: Electricity in the house. It makes things respond and move.

understand.keyTakeaway: HTML creates the skeleton, CSS makes it beautiful, JavaScript makes it interactive. You need HTML first — the other two are optional add-ons.

build.goal: See what each language does by removing them one at a time

codeComparison.old:
This heading is BIG and BLUE

This paragraph has a yellow background
codeComparison.new:
<!-- Try 1: Delete the <style> section entirely -->
<!-- Result: Page still works but looks plain and boring! -->
<!-- HTML works alone. CSS is optional. -->

<!-- Try 2: Undo, then delete onclick="..." from button -->
<!-- Result: Button looks pretty but doesn't DO anything! -->
<!-- JavaScript adds behavior. Also optional. -->

<!-- Key insight: HTML is the foundation. -->
<!-- CSS and JS are add-ons. -->

build.hint: Use Ctrl+Z (or Cmd+Z on Mac) to undo changes and get back to the working version.

challenge.title: Build a Language Comparison Page
challenge.mission: Create a page that clearly shows what each language does using THREE separate sections.
requirements: Section 1: HTML only (no CSS, no JS) — show plain structure
Section 2: HTML + CSS — show styled content
Section 3: HTML + CSS + JS — show interactive element
Each section has a heading explaining what it demonstrates
challenge.starterCode:
HTML Alone

This paragraph has no styling.

HTML + CSS

HTML + CSS + JavaScript
challenge.bonusChallenge: Make the JavaScript button change the text on the page instead of showing a popup. (Hint: Use document.getElementById() and .innerHTML)

capabilities: Explain the difference between HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
Build a page that demonstrates all three languages
Identify which language does what when reading code
Explain why HTML is NOT a programming language
Remove CSS or JS from a page and see what HTML does alone

bridge.preview: In Lesson 6, you'll see how browsers read your HTML code top-to-bottom and build a "tree" structure called the DOM. You'll make a page that visualizes this process!

lessons.title: Watch a Browser Build Your Page Step-by-Step

accomplishments: Create a page that shows HTML loading one piece at a time
See exactly how browsers read from top to bottom

do.title: Build a slow-loading page
steps: Create a new file called browser-reads-html.html
Copy and paste the code below
Watch the boxes appear one by one from top to bottom!
do.code:
📦 Box 1: I load FIRST (top of HTML)
📦 Box 2: I load SECOND
📦 Box 3: I load THIRD
📦 Box 4: I load LAST (bottom of HTML)
do.result: You just watched a browser read your HTML from top to bottom, exactly how it happens in real life (but slowed down so you can see it)!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing all four boxes after they have all appeared.

concepts.name: Top-to-Bottom Reading
concepts.definition: Browsers read HTML files starting at line 1 and moving down, line by line, until they reach the end. Just like reading a book.
concepts.analogy: Following a recipe. You can't skip to step 5 - you have to do steps 1, 2, 3, 4 first in order.

concepts.name: The Document Tree (DOM)
concepts.definition: As the browser reads, it builds a "tree" structure in memory. Your HTML becomes a family tree of elements: html is the grandparent, head and body are the parents, everything inside them are the children.
concepts.analogy: A family tree or an organization chart. Everything has a parent, and some things have children.

concepts.name: Why Order Matters
concepts.definition: If you put a <script> that tries to change an element BEFORE that element exists in the HTML, it won't work! The browser hasn't built it yet.
concepts.analogy: Trying to paint a wall that hasn't been built yet. You need the structure first.

concepts.name: The Critical Rendering Path
concepts.definition: The browser follows these steps: 1. Read HTML (top to bottom), 2. Build the DOM tree, 3. Apply CSS styles, 4. Paint pixels on screen, 5. Run JavaScript

understand.keyTakeaway: Browsers read your HTML top-to-bottom and build a tree structure (the DOM) as they go. Order matters!

build.goal: Break it on purpose — move a box before the <style> section and see what happens

codeComparison.old: Box 1
codeComparison.new: Box 1 (no styling!)

build.hint: This is supposed to break! The point is to SEE that order matters. Use Ctrl+Z to undo.

challenge.title: Build a DOM Tree Visualizer
challenge.mission: Create a visual family tree showing parent-child relationships in HTML.
requirements: Shows <html> at the top as the root
Uses indentation or visual lines to show the tree structure
Includes text explaining what each level is
challenge.starterCode:
My HTML DOM Tree

📄 <html> (root)
challenge.bonusChallenge: Use JavaScript to automatically generate the tree by reading document.documentElement (advanced!)

capabilities: Explain how browsers read HTML (top to bottom)
Understand why order matters in HTML
Debug issues caused by wrong element placement
Use the Console to check when the browser finishes loading

bridge.preview: In Lesson 7, you'll organize information into lists - perfect for making navigation menus, to-do lists, and step-by-step instructions.
